name: Hontanas from Castrojeriz via EuroVelo 3 — circular via Tamarón and Villaquirán de los Infantes
name_original: Hontanas desde Castrojeriz por EuroVelo 3 — circular por Tamarón y Villaquirán de los Infantes
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around Castrojeriz navigate a landscape shaped by the historic Camino de Santiago and the vast agricultural plains of the Meseta. The region features a mix of well-maintained dirt tracks and paved EuroVelo routes, often running parallel to the Camino. Prominent landmarks include the Castrojeriz Hill, crowned by its ancient castle, and the fertile river meadows of the Odra and Pisuerga rivers. The area is characterized by expansive wheat fields and unique calcareous moors, offering varied terrain for cyclists. Best touring cycling routes around Castrojeriz The most popular touring cycling route is Hontanas from Castrojeriz via EuroVelo 3 — circular via Tamarón and Villaquirán de los Infantes, a 24.3 miles (39.1 km) trail that takes 2 hours 39 minutes to complete. This moderate route follows sections of the EuroVelo 3, connecting charming villages. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Boadilla del Camino by EuroVelo 3 from Castrojeriz — circular by Astudillo and Castillo de Mota, a moderate 33.0 miles (53.0 km) path. This route offers a journey through historical sites and varied rural scenery along the EuroVelo 3. Local touring cyclists also love the Támara de Campos y Santoyo – circular desde Astudillo, a 20.7 miles (33.3 km) trail leading through agricultural plains and small towns, often completed in about 2 hours 47 minutes. Touring cycling around Castrojeriz is defined by agricultural plains, river valleys, and paths integrated with the Camino de Santiago. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate circular routes to more challenging long-distance trails. The routes in Castrojeriz are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.6 stars from over 20 reviews. More than 300 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Castrojeriz's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Castrojeriz?
A: There are over 19 touring cycling routes around Castrojeriz, offering a variety of experiences for different skill levels. These routes are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.6 stars from over 20 reviews.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes near Castrojeriz?
A: The terrain around Castrojeriz is diverse, featuring a mix of well-maintained dirt tracks, especially those integrated with the Camino de Santiago, and paved EuroVelo routes. You'll cycle through vast agricultural plains, which transform into a 'sea of golden cereals' in summer, and encounter fertile river meadows near the Odra and Pisuerga rivers. The region also includes unique calcareous moors and prominent 'witness hills' like the one crowned by Castrojeriz Castle.

Q: What are the difficulty levels of touring cycling routes in Castrojeriz?
A: The touring cycling routes in Castrojeriz cater to various abilities. You'll find 4 easy routes, 10 moderate routes, and 5 more challenging routes. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1378592370/hontanas-from-castrojeriz-via-eurovelo-3-circular-via-tamaron-and-villaquiran-de-los-infantes" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hontanas from Castrojeriz via EuroVelo 3&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate circular route, while the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38172163" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Burgos Cathedral – Convent of San Antón loop from Villaquirán&lt;/a&gt; offers a more difficult, longer challenge.

Q: What historical landmarks can I see while cycling near Castrojeriz?
A: The region is rich in history. You can cycle past the iconic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/848414" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ruins of the Monastery of San Antón&lt;/a&gt;, with its distinctive archway, or visit the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3930614" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Collegiate Church of Santa María del Manzano&lt;/a&gt;. The routes often pass through charming villages with medieval churches and historic town centers, offering glimpses into the region's past.

Q: Are there scenic viewpoints along the touring cycling routes?
A: Absolutely. The dramatic setting of Castrojeriz at the foot of its prominent hill, crowned by the ancient castle, offers panoramic views of the surrounding Meseta landscape. The climb out of Castrojeriz, especially at sunrise, provides breathtaking vistas. You can also find beautiful perspectives like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4567194" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Hontanas&lt;/a&gt; along some routes.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes around Castrojeriz?
A: Yes, several circular routes are available. A popular choice is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1378592370/hontanas-from-castrojeriz-via-eurovelo-3-circular-via-tamaron-and-villaquiran-de-los-infantes" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hontanas from Castrojeriz via EuroVelo 3&lt;/a&gt;, which is a moderate 39.1 km loop. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1378586983/boadilla-del-camino-by-eurovelo-3-from-castrojeriz-circular-by-astudillo-and-castillo-de-mota" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Boadilla del Camino by EuroVelo 3 from Castrojeriz&lt;/a&gt;, a 53 km circular path that takes you through historical sites.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in Castrojeriz?
A: The region is particularly beautiful in summer when the vast agricultural plains transform into a 'sea of golden cereals.' However, spring and autumn offer milder temperatures, which can be more comfortable for longer rides. Be aware that some dirt tracks, especially those on the Camino, can be challenging after rain.

Q: Are there family-friendly touring cycling routes in Castrojeriz?
A: Yes, there are 4 easy routes suitable for families or those looking for a more relaxed ride. These routes often follow well-maintained paths or quieter sections of the EuroVelo network, allowing for an enjoyable experience through the scenic countryside.

Q: Where can I find parking for touring cycling routes in Castrojeriz?
A: Castrojeriz, being a town on the Camino de Santiago, generally offers parking options within or on the outskirts of the town. Many villages along the routes also have designated parking areas, especially near churches or public squares, making it convenient to start your cycling adventure.

Q: Is public transport available to access touring cycling routes in Castrojeriz?
A: While Castrojeriz is a smaller town, it is connected by regional bus services that can facilitate access to the area. However, specific public transport options directly to trailheads might be limited. It's advisable to check local bus schedules for connections to Castrojeriz and nearby villages if you plan to use public transport.

Q: Are there places to eat or stay along the touring cycling routes?
A: As Castrojeriz is a significant stop on the Camino de Santiago, you'll find various amenities. The town itself has cafes, pubs, and accommodation options. Many of the smaller villages along the routes also offer pilgrim hostels (albergues) and local eateries, providing opportunities for refreshments and overnight stays. For example, you might find places like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5026850" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Albergue Juan de Yepes&lt;/a&gt; or &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4417930" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Albergue Fuente Sidres&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other touring cyclists say about the routes in Castrojeriz?
A: The touring cycling routes in Castrojeriz are highly regarded by the komoot community, boasting an impressive average rating of 4.6 stars from over 20 reviews. More than 300 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore the varied terrain, often praising the unique blend of historical sites, the peaceful agricultural landscapes, and the well-integrated sections of the Camino de Santiago and EuroVelo 3.
tips:
From the 13th century, and expanded in the 17th century. The Annunciation stands out with its Gothic façade and the main altarpiece with paintings by Mengs. Three naves, it contains altarpieces, tombs, paintings, images... the image of the Virgin of Manzano from the 13th century stands out.
On the main altar, a Rococo altarpiece stands out, whose central motif is the Annunciation by Mengs, chamber painter of Charles III. On the sides are the tombs of the Counts of Castro, Juan de Mendoza, Marquis of la Hinojosa, and Diego Sarmiento, Count of Ribadavia.

The Convento de San Anton is a former monastery of the Antoniter order near Castrojeriz. The Antoniter-Order had made it their task to heal sick people at the Antonius fire. It was founded in the 12th century by French monks. The now-abandoned buildings were built in the 14th century.

Church of the Immaculate Conception, dependent on the parish of Castrojeriz in the Archdiocese of Amaya, Diocese of Burgos

The parish church of Hontanas, presided over by a tall and strong tower, is dedicated to the Immaculate Conception. It was built primitively in Gothic style, in the fourteenth century, although neoclassical reformed with the passage of time. It houses a Baroque altarpiece by the mountaineer Fernando de la Peña. Inside there is a beautiful copper processional cross with enamel and figures from the thirteenth century, the style suggests that it is Spanish manufacture, late Romanesque style.
http://www.hontanas.es/lugares-de-interes/iglesia-parroquial-de-la-inmaculada-concepcion

Castrojeriz is a town with about 800 inhabitants and all services. That is why it is very relevant as a stop on the Camino de Santiago. In addition to enjoying the silhouette of the castle on top of the hill, it has several bars and restaurants, a fountain on the main street, a supermarket and even a municipal swimming pool in case it gets too hot.
